data.title: Hong Kong's Securities Industry Group Pushes Back As Regulators Plans Sweeping Crypto Licensing Crackdown
data.description:
Hong Kong Regulators Proposed To Scrap 10% Threshold, Industry Pushed Back

Hong Kong’s push to build a safe and tightly regulated digital asset market is running into resistance from traditional finance, as asset managers warn that proposed crypto licensing changes could make even modest exposure impractical.

In a submission dated Tuesday, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Professionals Association urged regulators to retain a long-standing “de minimis” exemption that allows Type 9 licensed portfolio managers to hold limited crypto exposure without triggering full virtual asset licensing. Under current rules, managers can allocate less than 10% of a fund’s gross asset value to virtual assets, provided they notify the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC).

Hong Kong’s securities industry group is pushing back on proposed crypto rules.

The HKSFPA says removing the 10% “de minimis” threshold would force fund managers to get a full crypto licence for even a 1% bitcoin allocation — a move it warns could deter traditional asset… pic.twitter.com/dACmxxrD2I

— Timmy Shen (@timmyhmshen) January 20, 2026

That threshold now sits at the center of the debate. Regulators have proposed scrapping the 10% carve-out entirely, a move that would require asset managers to obtain a full virtual asset management license even for small, experimental allocations. The industry group warned the change would force an “all-or-nothing” decision, replacing a risk-based framework with a blanket compliance burden.

According to the submission, the proposal is disproportionate to the level of risk involved. Managers seeking to test crypto as a portfolio diversifier would face significant licensing, custody and compliance costs before client demand is established, discouraging cautious participation rather than encouraging responsible adoption.

The association argued that a clear, risk-based exemption — backed by notification requirements rather than full licensing — would better align regulation with real-world portfolio management.

The pushback comes as Hong Kong accelerates its effort to formalize crypto oversight. In December, the Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau and the SFC published consultation conclusions on licensing for virtual asset dealing services, followed by a new public consultation covering advisory and asset management activities.

🇭🇰 NEW: Hong Kong moves forward with licensing requirements for virtual asset dealers and custodians, expanding its regulatory framework beyond stablecoin issuers. pic.twitter.com/0ZwIcEWiIG

— Cointelegraph (@Cointelegraph) December 25, 2025

Officials have framed the expansion as necessary to support institutional growth while ensuring investor protection and regulatory clarity.

Balancing Regulation And Market Growth

Custody rules have emerged as another pressure point. The association criticized proposals that would require virtual asset managers to use only SFC-licensed custodians, warning the mandate could prove unworkable for private equity and venture funds holding early-stage tokens that local custodians may not yet support. Industry participants say such constraints risk limiting legitimate investment strategies rather than reducing systemic risk.

Regulators argue the broader licensing architecture is designed to bring crypto activity onshore and integrate it into Hong Kong’s existing financial system, reducing reliance on informal interpretations. The industry, however, says the removal of the 10% threshold raises fixed costs so sharply that it may delay — rather than deepen — institutional participation.

HONG KONG, March 2025 – The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Professionals Association (HKSFPA) has launched a significant challenge against proposed international cryptocurrency reporting standards, urging substantial modifications to the OECD'shttps://t.co/5cwtcwaj2gpic.twitter.com/o7WQP7V04M

— BitcoinWorld Media (@ItsBitcoinWorld) January 19, 2026

At stake is how quickly traditional fund managers can move from observation to allocation. Maintaining the carve-out lowers barriers for stock and bond managers to gain limited exposure to assets like Bitcoin, while eliminating it effectively confines crypto to firms willing to commit to full-scale licensing from the outset.

The government has said it will refine the proposals based on industry feedback before introducing legislation in 2026, leaving room for compromise as Hong Kong balances regulatory rigor with its ambition to become a leading Asian hub for digital assets.

data.title: NYSE announces tokenized securities trading platform: Will the rules of the game for US stocks change?
data.description:
Deng Tong, Jinse Finance

On January 19, 2026, the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE): part of the Intercontinental Exchange Group (NYSE: ICE) announced that it is developing a platform for trading and on-chain settlement of tokenized securities and will seek regulatory approval for this purpose.

I. NYSE Develops Tokenized Trading and Settlement Platform for US Stocks

The NYSE's new digital platform will support a tokenized trading experience, including 24/7 operation, instant settlement, USD-denominated orders, and stablecoin-based funding. The platform is designed to integrate the NYSE's advanced Pillar matching engine and blockchain-based post-trade processing system, and supports multi-chain settlement and custody.

Upon regulatory approval, the platform will provide the NYSE with a new trading venue supporting the trading of tokenized stocks interchangeable with traditional securities, as well as natively issued digital security tokens. Tokenized shareholders will enjoy traditional shareholder dividends and corporate governance rights.

The trading venue is designed in accordance with established market structure principles and is distributed to all qualified broker-dealers through non-discriminatory channels. The launch of the New York Stock Exchange's tokenized securities platform is part of the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)'s broader digital strategy, which also includes improving its clearing infrastructure to support 24/7 trading and potential tokenized collateral consolidation. Currently, ICE is partnering with several banks, including Bank of New York (NYSE: BK) and Citibank (NYSE: C), to support tokenized deposits at ICE's clearing houses, helping clearing members transfer and manage funds outside of traditional banking hours, meet margin requirements, and adapt to the funding needs of different jurisdictions and time zones. Lynn Martin, President of the New York Stock Exchange Group, stated, “For over two centuries, the New York Stock Exchange has revolutionized how markets operate. We are leading the industry toward fully on-chain solutions, thanks to our unparalleled security and high-standard regulatory framework, enabling us to seamlessly combine trust with cutting-edge technology. We will leverage our expertise to reshape market infrastructure to meet and shape the needs of the digital future.” Michael Broglund, Vice President of Strategic Planning at ICE, stated, “Since its inception, ICE has been committed to driving the transformation of markets from analog to digital. Supporting tokenized securities is a key step for ICE in operating its on-chain market infrastructure in the new era of global finance, which will be used for trading, settlement, custody, and capital formation.” Tokenized shares are shares of traditional corporate stock minted on a blockchain ledger, providing investors with stock price exposure and advantages such as 24/7 access and partial ownership. According to the proposal, trading could use stablecoins for real-time financing and settlement, instead of the current one-day settlement cycle used in the US stock market. To meet global demand for US stocks, the New York Stock Exchange has sought to extend weekday trading hours to 22 hours. In October 2024, the New York Stock Exchange announced plans to submit an application to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to extend trading hours. In March 2025, Nasdaq also announced plans to implement 24-hour trading on weekdays.

II. What prompted the NYSE to take action?

The most fundamental reason is the favorable U.S. cryptocurrency regulatory policies.

In June 2025, the GENIUS Act was passed and officially became law on July 18, creating the first federal regulatory system for stablecoins. Strong reserve requirements ensure the stability and trustworthiness of stablecoins. The GENIUS Act plays a key role in attracting more digital asset activity to the United States by providing clear rules and promoting responsible innovation in the stablecoin market.

For details, please see the Jinse Finance article "White House: Historic Legislation - President Trump Signs GENIUS Act"

In December 2025, Depository Trust Company (DTC) received a No-Agreement Letter from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), allowing it to tokenize some of its custodied assets. DTC received the SEC's No-Agreement Letter (NAL), authorizing it to provide a new service in a controlled production environment, within the framework of federal securities laws and regulations, for tokenizing real-world assets held by DTC. DTC expects to launch this service in the second half of 2026.

With DTC's approval to tokenize core assets such as stocks, bonds, and ETFs on-chain, real-world assets are being formally incorporated into the U.S. federal securities system.

This means that core asset classes in traditional financial markets will have "native versions" on the blockchain in the future, enjoying all the legal rights of traditional assets. For details, please see the Jinse Finance article "Frequent Regulatory Benefits from the US SEC and OCC: What are the Implications?" The New York Stock Exchange platform will adopt "stablecoin-based financing," meaning that the US dollar itself is flowing into the crypto market. Bernstein predicts that by the end of 2026, the supply of stablecoins will reach $420 billion. III. Who Else is Tokenizing? Nasdaq-listed tokenization startups and the New York Stock Exchange both rely on the same stablecoin track and blockchain settlement system. Once assets are on-chain, any application can present them. Although distribution channels are decentralized, the underlying architecture tends to be unified. The competition for "universal applications" in the financial field is actually a competition to build the best shared track interface.

Undoubtedly, global financial infrastructure is shifting towards the crypto space.

1. BlackRock

BlackRock's BUIDL is one of the most iconic institutional-grade RWA tokenized products to date. This product issues US dollar money market fund units as tokens on a public blockchain, with underlying assets primarily consisting of short-term US Treasury bonds and cash equivalents.

Its core innovation lies not in the assets themselves, but in the fact that fund units exist as on-chain tokens; it supports on-chain transfer, staking, and combined use; and through compliant custody and auditing mechanisms, it achieves the same level of regulation and security as traditional funds. BUIDL is widely regarded as the prototype of an "on-chain risk-free rate," building a crucial bridge between DeFi and traditional finance.

2. Franklin Templeton

Franklin Templeton was one of the first traditional asset management institutions to directly put the Government Money Market Fund Registration System (GMF) on-chain.

Using its self-developed Benji platform, the entire process of fund share registration, transfer, and settlement is on-chain, while maintaining SEC compliance, pioneering the on-chain transformation of traditional money market funds. Its tokenized fund achieves on-chain registration and transfer of fund shares; automated dividend distribution and information disclosure; and a dual-track structure parallel to the traditional regulatory system. It further adapts to stablecoin reserves and on-chain distribution scenarios, deepening the integration of traditional funds and digital finance. 3. Citi and SIX In May 2025, global banking giant Citibank partnered with SIX Digital Exchange (SDX), the digital asset arm of a major Swiss securities exchange, to tokenize unlisted shares. Citibank will act as custodian and issuing agent, providing tokenized versions of late-stage, unlisted company equity on SDX's regulated blockchain platform—the Central Securities Depository (CSD). 4. Coinbase On January 16th, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong stated that although Coinbase entered the stock market relatively late, the company is making long-term plans. He believes that Coinbase, with its deep cryptocurrency expertise and the most trusted crypto brand, will become a bridge connecting traditional finance and cryptocurrency, and drive the development of tokenized stocks. Tokenized stocks refer to digital tokens representing real-world shares of companies like Apple and Tesla. Their advantage lies in their ability to be traded across exchanges and settled instantly. Brian Armstrong disclosed that Coinbase's backend stock tokenization business is currently supported by Apex Fintech Solutions, a service that is temporarily only open to a limited number of users, but plans to expand to all customers in the coming weeks. 5. Robinhood In late June 2025, Robinhood launched blockchain-based tokenized US stocks and ETFs in the European market (EU/EEA). Robinhood launched its tokenized equity product based on the Ethereum Layer 2 network Arbitrum for European users, covering more than 200 US stocks and ETFs, supporting 24/7 trading on weekdays. Robinhood CEO Vlad Tenev stated, "Being the first platform to actually tokenize these great companies is a significant milestone. Whenever you pioneer something meaningful, there will always be some controversy." He plans to expand this model to thousands of private companies and integrate it with the DeFi market. 6. Bank of New York On January 9th, the world's largest custodian bank, Bank of New York (BNY), took its first step towards tokenization, offering institutional clients the ability to settle bank deposits on the blockchain. The Wall Street giant (managing nearly $58 trillion in assets) is launching a platform that reflects customer balances on a private blockchain to support faster settlement and liquidity management. Carolyn Weinberg, Chief Product and Innovation Officer at Bank of New York, stated, “Tokenized deposits give us the opportunity to extend trusted bank deposits to digital channels—enabling customers to process collateral, margin, and payments faster within a framework built for scale, resilience, and regulatory consistency.” 7. State Street Bank On January 15th, State Street Bank announced the launch of a series of tokenized products. The bank will develop tokenized money market funds, exchange-traded funds (ETFs), as well as cash products such as tokenized deposits and stablecoins. Joerg Ambrosius, President of Investment Services at State Street Bank, stated that the launch of the digital asset platform is an important step in State Street Bank's strategy. State Street Bank currently oversees $51.7 trillion in assets and has previously provided management and accounting services for crypto ETFs. Last December, it also partnered with Galaxy Digital to create a tokenized fund and will consider providing custody services in the future, depending on regulatory developments. 8. Bank of New York Mellon On January 9th, Bank of New York Mellon launched a tokenized deposit service, allowing customers to transfer funds via a blockchain network. This tokenized deposit is an on-chain representation of deposits held in a customer's bank account, usable for collateral and margin trading, and operates 24/7 to expedite payments. Currently participating institutions include Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), Citadel Securities, DRW Holdings, Ripple Prime, Baillie Gifford, and Circle. 9. Streamex In July 2025, BioSig Technologies signed a definitive agreement with Streamex for up to $1.1 billion in funding to finance its gold-backed treasury model and expand its tokenization platform for real-world assets (RWAs) such as gold and other commodities. Streamex plans to issue tokens backed by gold and other commodities through its platform built on the Solana (SOL) blockchain. In November of last year, Streamex announced the completion of a $25 million initial convertible bond financing round, with Cantor, Clear Street, and Needham & Company, LLC acting as placement agents. The company will use the funds to purchase physical gold, thereby strengthening Streamex's gold tokenization plan and maintaining its gold-backed balance sheet strategy. IV. What Has Tokenization Bringed? Compared to the crypto market, the US stock market is clearly lagging behind. The US stock market still relies on a hierarchical structure designed for the pre-digital era. Trading, clearing, settlement, and custody are handled by different entities, each maintaining its own ledger. This structure presents several problems: funds are tied up during settlement windows, counterparty risk persists until the trade is fully cleared, and reconciliation between intermediaries increases costs and operational risks. Most importantly, despite the continuous flow of information globally, the market remains constrained by fixed trading hours. These issues are impacting market volatility, liquidity, and market behavior. Tokenization directly addresses these inefficiencies. With digital ledgers, ownership updates and settlements can be completed near real-time. Simultaneously, capital efficiency is improved because collateral and cash are no longer locked up while awaiting transaction clearing. For institutions, this impacts their balance sheets. For the market as a whole, it simplifies increasingly complex post-trade processing procedures. Crucially, tokenization does not change the nature of stocks; it changes how the system handles stock ownership. V. What Industry Insiders Say Duke University Finance Professor Campbell Harvey points out: “Tokenization is not a trend. It’s a technology that solves problems. And it opens up many possibilities; tokenized stocks may be the most easily achievable goal.” CZ responded to the “New York Stock Exchange announces the launch of a tokenized platform” by saying, “This is good news for cryptocurrencies and cryptocurrency trading platforms.” Superstate founder Robert Leshner stated that long-standing legal and operational barriers are beginning to change. “With the emergence of credible, issuer-led on-chain equity structures, public equity has gone from ‘prohibited from trading’ to ‘tradable’.” A number of trading platforms, including Robinhood, Kraken, and Gemini, have begun offering tokenized versions of the most popular stocks. The trend of moving from encapsulated synthetic assets to directly issued tokens is also accelerating.

data.title: Silver Fever Hits India as Imports Jump 400% and Nifty Metal Index Outshines Market
data.description:
Silver has long been a staple of Indian households, but the scale of recent acquisitions has moved beyond traditional gifting to a full-scale industrial and financial surge.

In just four months, India has moved $5.9 billion worth of the metal across its borders.

This represents a staggering 400% increase compared to the end of 2024 and towers over the previous 2022 record by 64%.

To put this into perspective, the country typically averaged just $1.5 billion in annual imports between 2013 and 2019.

India is stockpiling silver:

India's silver imports spiked to a record ~$5.9 billion over the last 4 months.

This marks a +400% increase since Q4 2024 and is now +64% above the 2022 peak.

By comparison, imports averaged ~$1.5 billion per year from 2013 to 2019.

India is one… pic.twitter.com/RIBmwImFBj

— The Kobeissi Letter (@KobeissiLetter) January 19, 2026

Why Is India Buying So Much Silver?

This massive appetite is being fueled by a triple threat of demand: traditional jewellery enthusiasts, investors stacking physical bars, and a booming industrial sector.

As India expands its green energy footprint, silver has become an essential component in the manufacturing of solar panels and high-end electronics.

While India has historically been a dominant force in the global silver market, the current pace of buying suggests a strategic shift toward the metal as both a hedge and a necessity for modern infrastructure.

Source: Pixabay

Are Metal Stocks Outperforming The Broader Market?

The enthusiasm for physical silver has spilled directly into the equity markets.

The Nifty Metal Index, which tracks the heavyweights of the mining and metals sectors, recently recorded its strongest opening quarter since 2018.

This sector is currently outpacing the benchmark Nifty 50, pushing the metals-to-main-index ratio to an 11-year peak.

🔥🚨👀 Metals are outperforming the Nifty 50 by the widest margin in almost 11 years

And it looks like this outperformance has only just begun
(Not a prediction, Just an observation) pic.twitter.com/R0k4SsUKOG

— Decoded (@Decoded0001) January 20, 2026

Investors are clearly pivoting toward industrial assets, leaving traditional blue-chip stocks in the rearview mirror.

Will Record Global Prices Dampen Domestic Demand?

On the international stage, silver prices have been nothing short of explosive.

Spot silver recently climbed over 5% to reach an all-time high of $94.68 an ounce, marking a 32% rise since the start of January 2026.

#Silver $94.680 +4.74 +5.27%
Some serious goal tending going on for most of the day At the $94.50 range...the powers that be absolutely do not want silver to breach $100 an ounce like it has weeks ago in China...FYI the price in a store in China is $120 pic.twitter.com/lh2GV5C8k9

— StockShaman ⚒ #PeakCopper AKA Greg Shafransky 🍌 (@StockShaman) January 19, 2026

However, this global peak has created a strange paradox in India.

Local demand has begun to cool at these price levels, leading to a rare situation where physical silver is trading at a significant discount.

Surendra Mehta, national secretary of the India Bullion and Jewellers Association (IBJA), explained,

“There is a ₹10,000 per kilo (kg) discount for bars prevailing in the physical market here.”

While the landed cost for dealers stood at ₹302,628 per kg, Mumbai markets were offering the metal at ₹292,628.

This suggests that while the world is screaming "buy," local physical buyers are starting to catch their breath.

How Are Silver Etfs Performing For Investors?

For those not wanting to carry heavy bars, paper silver has proven to be a goldmine.

The Nippon India Silver ETF saw a 5.66% jump to ₹284.70 per gram, bringing its year-to-date returns to 32.45%.

Over the last 12 months, the fund has delivered a massive 225% return.

#Silver Vs #Silverbees
Handsome return 180% within 1.5 year pic.twitter.com/bdE0o6zWhT

— 𝐒𝐚𝐧𝐣𝐚𝐲 𝐆𝐚𝐫𝐰𝐚 (@garwasanjay) January 19, 2026

This stands in sharp contrast to the Nifty index, which dipped 2.15% in the same period and managed only a 10.3% gain over the past year.

This performance has triggered a flood of capital; ETF assets managed by Kotak Mutual Fund swelled from ₹15,339.21 crore in March 2025 to ₹72,907.44 crore by December 2025.

Is Industrial Production Keeping Up With The Rally?

The financial windfall is clearly visible in corporate earnings.

Hindustan Zinc, which commands roughly 75% of India’s zinc market, reported a 46.2% surge in Q3 profits, reaching ₹39.16 billion.

This growth was supported by a 27.5% rise in revenue, with mined and refined production hitting record highs for the October to December quarter.

While silver is the star of the show, other precious metals are also finding their footing.

Gold rose 1.7% to $4,672.49 per ounce, while platinum and palladium saw gains of 1.5% and 1.1% respectively.

With silver futures on the MCX recently surging 5.5% to hit ₹3.03 lakh per kg, the metal continues to be the primary engine driving India's commodity markets into 2026.

data.title: What should we "go all in" on in 2026?
data.description:
Author: Frank, MSX Research Institute

At the beginning of 2026, the world stood at a deeply divided crossroads.

On one hand, inflation was declining, AI was rapidly penetrating, and capital markets were stirring; on the other hand, geopolitical frictions were escalating, institutional uncertainty was rising, and there was widespread skepticism about whether the next round of growth truly existed. Against this backdrop, the globally influential technology and business podcast, *All-In Podcast*, released its ultimate annual prediction:

Led by renowned Silicon Valley angel investor (early investor in Uber and Robinhood) Jason Calacanis, the podcast featured three heavyweight guests: Chamath, the "King of SPACs"; David Friedberg, the "Sultan of Science"; and David Sacks, dubbed the White House's first "AI and Cryptocurrency Czar."

This group of top minds, controlling hundreds of billions of dollars and deeply versed in the logic of power and capital operations, engaged in a fiery debate surrounding the political, technological, investment, and geopolitical landscape—from California's wealth tax crisis to expectations of 6% GDP growth, from optimism about Huawei/market predictions to the startling hypothesis that SpaceX might merge with Tesla, and much more. Maiton MSX Research Institute has distilled the core viewpoints of their intellectual exchange for readers.

11 Predictions from 4 Industry Leaders

Source: AI-generated image

Regarding California's wealth tax and the risk of capital outflow, they respectively believe:

  • Chamath Palihapitiya: He has clearly chosen to leave California's elite circles, with a combined net worth of hundreds of billions of dollars, which constitutes a substantial impact on California's long-term finances;

  • David Friedberg: The proposal is unlikely to be implemented, but it has already exposed the structural pressures on local finances; David Sacks: A wealth tax was the direct reason I left California. Even if it doesn't pass in 2026, everyone expects some version to come back in 2028; Regarding the biggest business winners in 2026, they believe: Jason Calacanis: He is optimistic about Amazon, which will be the first to experience the "corporate singularity," becoming the first company where robots contribute more profit than humans. Its automated warehouses and logistics network have built a very high moat; Chamath Palihapitiya: He chooses copper, as geopolitics and supply chain security will cause a long-term supply-demand imbalance. At the current rate, by By 2040, the global copper supply will face a deficit of approximately 70%; David Friedberg: Bullish on Huawei and prediction markets (PM), the former continues to make breakthroughs in its technological system, while the latter is evolving from a peripheral product into a new type of infrastructure for information and price discovery, potentially experiencing explosive growth this year; David Sacks: 2026 will be a big year for IPOs, with the "Trump boom" restarting the capital market expansion cycle, resulting in a large number of successful IPOs and creating trillions of dollars in new market capitalization; He also agrees with Jason Calacanis's assessment of Amazon, but for different reasons (not elaborated); Regarding the biggest business losers in 2026, they respectively believe: Jason Calacanis: The most impacted will be young white-collar workers in the US, with recent graduates being prioritized for replacement by AI and automation; Chamath Palihapitiya: The "maintenance and migration" revenue model for enterprise SaaS will be systematically compressed under the impact of AI; David Friedberg: State government finances, pension debt and solvency issues will be exposed; David Sacks: In California, regulatory and tax uncertainties will continue to squeeze out capital and businesses; Regarding the most significant deal in 2026, they believe: Jason Calacanis: There will be an AI deal exceeding $50 billion. Mergers and acquisitions; Chamath Palihapitiya: Predicts that traditional mergers and acquisitions will give way to large-scale IP licensing collaborations, a type of transaction that will become more common and mature in 2026; David Friedberg: Geopolitical conflict resolution will be the "biggest deal," with the Russia-Ukraine conflict potentially resolved this year; David Sacks: Bullish on the growth of the coding assistants and tool use sectors; Regarding the boldest contrarian predictions for 2026, they believe: Jason Calacanis: A substantial easing of US-China relations will occur, and the two sides will reach a win-win working relationship; Chamath Palihapitiya: Two contrarian predictions: First, SpaceX will not IPO, but may instead merge into Tesla; second, central banks will build a completely new paradigm for sovereign cryptocurrencies (different from BTC); David Friedberg: If the Iranian crisis continues to deepen, it may exacerbate instability in the Middle East; David Sacks: AI will become a job expander, not a job-creating machine, and we are likely to see job growth; Regarding the best-performing assets in 2026, they believe: Jason Calacanis: Bullish on platform assets such as speculative investments. In an environment where the economy is poised for takeoff, interest rates may fall, and people have more disposable income, people will have more money to bet and speculate on. Chamath Palihapitiya: Continue to bet on a basket of key metals, including copper. David Friedberg: Prediction markets are replacing the functions of traditional media and markets and have huge potential. David Sacks: Opt for the technology expansion supercycle. Regarding the worst-performing assets in 2026, they believe: Jason Calacanis: The US dollar will continue to be under pressure. Chamath Palihapitiya: Judges oil prices to be in a long-term downward trend, potentially falling to $45 per barrel; David Friedberg: Bearish on Netflix and traditional media stocks; David Sacks: Bearish on California high-end real estate; Regarding the most anticipated trends for 2026, they respectively believe: Jason Calacanis: The IPO market will return to its former glory; at least two of the giants like SpaceX, Anthropic, or OpenAI will file for IPOs this year; Chamath Palihapitiya: Expects the expansion of "Trumpism," unilateralism, and economic resilience—a huge trend that will result in massive GDP growth. Growth; David Friedberg: The reshaping of the Middle East landscape brought about by the deepening situation in Iran; David Sacks: Auditing government spending at all levels requires normalizing the "decentralized DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency)" to let the public see where money is being spent; Regarding the biggest political winners of 2026: Jason Calacanis: Young left-wing politicians; Chamath Palihapitiya: Anti-waste and anti-bureaucratic political forces; David Friedberg: Democratic Socialists (DSA) are taking over the Democratic Party, and this trend will continue in 2026. The year is being consolidated; David Sacks: "Trump Prosperity," predicting a 75 to 100 basis point rate cut in June; Regarding the biggest political losers in 2026: Jason Calacanis: Democratic centrist; Chamath Palihapitiya: The Monroe Doctrine—because Trumpism has triumphed over it; David Friedberg: The tech industry, becoming a common target for both left and right populism; David Sacks: Democratic centrist; Regarding 2026 Forecast of US GDP growth rate for 2025: Chamath Palihapitiya: Lower limit is 5%, upper limit is 6.2%; David Friedberg: 4.6%; David Sacks: 5%; In conclusion, China also released its 2025 national economic performance report today, showing a GDP of 140.19 trillion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 5.0%, achieving its target as scheduled. If we broaden our perspective to a global scale and consider exchange rate factors over the next one to two years, we'll find that the GDP gap between China and the US (in dollar terms), which widened significantly in the previous two years, seems to be showing subtle signs of narrowing again at this juncture. This contrast is quite intriguing: on one hand, China is seeking high-quality growth through structural adjustments; on the other hand, as described in the *All-In Podcast*, the US is attempting to forcefully escape its period of mediocre growth through "Trump prosperity + AI singularity." It can be said that the world's only two major economies are simultaneously entering a re-competition phase centered on productivity and structural efficiency. It is against this backdrop that Chamath Palihapitiya's statement on the show appears particularly provocative: "Don't short the US economy; it's ready to take off. 6% GDP growth is not a pipe dream." But the prerequisite is that, in this year of accelerated reshuffling, you must be on the side of productivity, not on the side of being eliminated. Perhaps this is the most important issue in this cycle. Let us strive together.

data.title: Pantera 2025 Review: What Structural Developments Have Occurred in the Crypto Industry?
data.description: The biggest change in US crypto regulation in 2025 is not the advancement of any new rule or bill, but rather the redefinition of the overall governance approach. From the White House's executive push to the Presidential Digital Assets Task Force providing a systematic roadmap, and then to the synchronized adjustments in enforcement stance, priorities, and focus by the SEC and CFTC, a more framework-driven regulatory cycle has officially begun. For the cryptocurrency industry, this means that the high degree of uncertainty of the past, requiring guessing regulatory attitudes, is being replaced by clearer asset classifications, rule paths, and registration mechanisms. In 2025, the entire market was greatly encouraged, with more and more companies and entrepreneurs choosing onshore issuance and compliant operations again. The trend towards industry standardization, institutionalization, and compliance is continuously strengthening. To this end, we have compiled an in-depth research report from Pantera, a leading Web3 VC, which provides a detailed review of all important administrative, regulatory, and legislative measures in the United States throughout 2025, and constructs a systemic framework for understanding US crypto regulatory practices for your reference. TLDR: Core Logic Chain Outline 1. Administrative-Driven Regulatory Shift Changes in Government Preferences → Systematic Recommendations from the President's Digital Asset Task Force → Regulatory Agencies Adjust Their Stance and Enforcement Focus According to the Roadmap 2. SEC's Shift from Enforcement-Driven to Framework-Driven Past Case-Based Boundary Delineation Caused Uncertainty → New Leadership Emphasizes Classification and Rule-Based Pathways → Market Expectations Return to Onshore Issuance and Compliance Operations 3. Four asset classifications reduce friction at the issuance end, but compliance boundaries still require auditable standards. Breaking down digital assets into commodities, collectibles, tools, and security tokens → provides a map for the compliant path of issuance and trading → alleviates the forced shutdown of US market participants due to compliance. 4. Stablecoin legislation provides certainty but also locks up part of the competitive structure. The scaling up of stablecoins touches on payments and financial stability → the GENIUS Act establishes a licensing system and reserve constraints → institutional funds are more willing to enter, but the space for innovation is compressed by the prudent framework. 5. Market structure legislation determines the long-term pricing power. The industry needs to clarify the regulatory models and boundaries of the CFTC and SEC → The CLARITY Act attempts to delineate digital goods from restricted assets → If the licensing combination of exchanges, brokerages, custody, and issuance is reshaped… 6. Prediction markets are moving from the periphery to financial infrastructure, but uncertainty in state-level regulation is the biggest obstacle. Kalshi establishes a DCM compliance path at the federal level → Traditional platforms enter the market and expand their products → Accelerated integration of prediction markets and crypto infrastructure. 7. Withdrawal of enforcement cases and reorganization of entities change risk distribution. Withdrawal of multiple major lawsuits reduces systemic regulatory pressure on the industry → Enforcement agencies shift focus to fraud and misconduct → Compliance focus shifts from lack of licenses to market integrity and disclosure quality. 8. RWA Tokenization Enters the Core of Traditional Securities On-chain ledger and programmability improve asset circulation efficiency → RWA expands from government bonds and commodities to fund shares and stocks → NASDAQ and others will further promote tokenized securities trading, forcing the SEC to provide a clear compliance path. Based on the content of this in-depth research report, we can give several relatively clear judgments and conclusions: The essence of the regulatory shift is from crackdown to governance, but the result of governance is the reshaping of the distribution of interests. With improved compliance clarity, the winners are often institutional platforms that can quickly build a closed loop of licensing, custody, clearing, and distribution. Meanwhile, the rapid entry of traditional financial institutions is an inevitable trend. Stablecoin legislation brings certainty to institutional funding, while simultaneously reducing the space for platform subsidies and revenue-generating innovation. On the surface, stablecoin regulation is about risk control; at a deeper level, it's about repositioning the competitive landscape on the payment and liability sides. The CLARITY Market Structure Act determines the long-term pricing power and industry dominance, leading to a restructuring of the compliance systems for exchanges, brokerages, and custody services. Many previously thriving gray-area businesses will become unsustainable due to high compliance costs. The key variable in the RWA industry has shifted from technology to regulatory auditability. Whoever can standardize disclosure, registration, custody, settlement, and investor suitability into compliant modules is more likely to capture the incremental market share of tokenized securities. I. The Presidential Working Group on Digital Asset Markets Within days of taking office, President Trump signed an executive order requiring "regulatory clarity" for crypto assets. This order established the "Presidential Working Group on Digital Asset Markets," chaired by David Sacks, head of AI and crypto affairs, with members including the Secretary of the Treasury, the Chairman of the SEC, the Chairman of the CFTC, and other agency and department heads. The working group's role is to review existing regulations and propose regulatory reforms to promote the development of crypto assets. In July, the working group released a comprehensive report entitled "Strengthening American Leadership in Digital Financial Technology." The report makes 100 policy and legislative recommendations, covering issues such as digital asset market structure, banking and digital assets, stablecoins and payments, combating illicit finance, and taxation. Among other things, the report clearly distinguishes between "digital assets that are securities" (regulated by the SEC) and "non-securities digital assets" (regulated by the CFTC). This marks a significant shift from the Biden-era SEC, which tended to classify most crypto assets as securities. As described below, the SEC and CFTC have begun taking action to implement the report's recommendations. II. The SEC, the Crypto Task Force, and "Project Crypto" Within days of President Trump's inauguration, then-Acting SEC Chairman Mark Uyeda established the "Crypto Task Force" within the Commission, aiming to develop a comprehensive and clear regulatory framework for crypto assets. Led by Commissioner Hester Peirce, the task force's goal was to create a reasonable and feasible regulatory path, unlike the SEC's previous approach, which was perceived as primarily driven by enforcement actions. In August, SEC Chairman Paul Atkins delivered a landmark speech, declaring that most crypto assets are not securities and announcing the launch of a program called "Project Crypto." Chairman Atkins outlined five core elements of the program: Establishing a clear regulatory framework for crypto asset issuance in the United States; Ensuring market participants have the freedom to choose between crypto exchanges and crypto custodians. Embrace market competition and promote the "super-apps" model: allowing platforms and intermediaries to offer a variety of crypto services and assets—covering both securities and non-securities—under a single, efficient licensing structure. Support on-chain innovation and decentralized finance (DeFi). Promote innovation exemptions and commercial viability arrangements. III. ICOs, Securities Attributes, and Crypto Asset Issuance: Has the Light Finally Appeared? For participants in the US crypto market, the biggest regulatory risks often focus on whether a particular crypto asset constitutes a security, or whether its offering to US investors constitutes a securities transaction. The previous administration and the SEC under former Chairman Gary Gensler tended to treat most crypto assets as securities and adopted a strategy often referred to as "regulation by enforcement" towards crypto issuers and other market participants. This path has prompted many crypto asset issuers to move offshore, issuing assets through foundations in jurisdictions such as the Cayman Islands and Panama; many crypto exchanges are screening US users, and many crypto companies are reducing or even terminating their interactions with the US market. The SEC under Chairman Atkins has taken a drastically different approach. The current SEC has withdrawn several cases against crypto platforms and issuers and proposed a more lenient new classification system, dividing crypto assets into four categories: Digital Commodities: whose value is related to the functionality of a working, decentralized protocol, rather than relying on management commitments or the issuer's ongoing efforts. Digital Collectibles: Tokens intended for collection purposes, such as NFTs. Digital Tools: Tokens with practical uses, such as access rights, credentials, or identity functions. Tokenized Securities: Tokenized forms representing traditional securities or financial instruments (such as equity or debt), still subject to securities laws. Before the SEC Chairman formally proposed these four frameworks, SEC staff had already hinted at this approach through no-action letters and related statements. During 2025, SEC staff issued guidance stating that fiat-denominated US stablecoins and memecoins are not securities; protocol staking and liquidity staking do not create securities. There is ample reason to believe that in 2026, the SEC will continue to adopt a relatively lenient approach to crypto regulation and gradually establish regulatory framework elements for the issuance of online tokens and other crypto assets within the country. IV. The Rise of Prediction Markets In 2025, prediction markets also experienced a surge in popularity and gradually gained greater regulatory acceptance. Prediction market platforms allow users to express their views on real-world outcomes through "event contracts": Contracts are based on event outcomes; the winner receives the full value of the contract, while the loser receives nothing. A key turning point occurred when Kalshi—one of the earliest operating prediction markets in the US—won its regulatory battle with the CFTC, gaining permission to operate as a CFTC-regulated "Designated Contract Market" and offering contracts linked to elections and other events. Following Kalshi's victory, market interest in prediction markets has surged: more platforms have received federal approval, and traditional financial and consumer internet platforms (such as Robinhood) have entered the field. Although regulatory stances remain uneven—especially under the gambling laws of some states—prediction markets are increasingly being seen as a legitimate financial "primitive." Notably, several platforms are exploring tokenization or crypto-based implementations, further integrating prediction markets with digital asset infrastructure. Coinbase's announcement of a partnership with Kalshi underscores this trend and is likely to accelerate further in 2026. V. Important Litigation Updates Withdrawal of Lawsuits Against Coinbase and Other Crypto-Related Companies — In 2023, the SEC filed a major lawsuit against Coinbase in the Southern District of New York and against Binance in the District of Columbia, alleging that they operated as unregistered broker-dealers, exchanges, and clearinghouses, and that they conducted unregistered securities offerings through their respective staking services. In the first quarter of 2025, the SEC reached joint stipulations with Coinbase and Binance, respectively, withdrawing all charges against them. (Pantera had predicted this development in a previous annual update.) The Commission also withdrew pending enforcement actions against other crypto market participants, including Kraken, Consensys, Ripple, and DRW Cumberland. The SEC stated that withdrawing these pending enforcement actions was part of its ongoing effort to reform the way it regulates the crypto industry and was not based on any judgment of the merits of the allegations against the entities involved. The SEC's new "Cyber ​​and Emerging Technologies Unit" – a reform of its crypto enforcement efforts – is also reflected in the establishment of this new unit, replacing the previous "Crypto Assets and Cyber ​​Unit," which led numerous enforcement actions against prominent crypto market players. The new unit is expected to focus more on fraud and other misconduct, including blockchain-related fraud and fraud using emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence and machine learning. VI. Outlook: Consolidating Crypto Policy Achievements Under the Trump Administration While the shift in the crypto policy environment is real and clear, several regulatory and legislative developments warrant close monitoring as we head towards 2026. We highlight several key areas below: 1. The GENIUS Act When discussing 2025, the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins (GENIUS Act) is indispensable—it is the first major federal-level crypto legislation. The bill, passed with bipartisan support, established a regulatory framework for “payment stablecoins.” Under the bill, issuers of payment stablecoins are generally restricted to: (1) certain qualified U.S. entities subject to federal regulation (or, for some issuers, state regulation); or (2) certain qualified non-U.S. entities registered with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) and subject to a “comparable regulatory and oversight mechanism” (as determined by the Secretary of the Treasury). The bill sets licensing requirements for issuers and imposes prudential supervision and consumer protection standards similar to those applied to banks, aiming to improve the transparency of reserve assets and mitigate perceived risks. Payment-type stablecoins do not include "algorithmic stablecoins"; furthermore, issuers of payment-type stablecoins are prohibited from paying interest to stablecoin holders. The public comment period for the bill has begun, and clauses such as the "prohibition of issuers paying interest to holders" are expected to generate strong controversy. 2. Comprehensive Crypto Legislation: Market Structure Act Unlike the GENIUS Act, comprehensive crypto market structure legislation is still progressing intermittently in Congress. The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act of 2025, commonly known as the CLARITY Act, passed the House of Representatives in July 2025 with strong bipartisan support, but has yet to make progress in the Senate. This bill proposes to divide jurisdiction between the CFTC and the SEC: the CFTC will regulate "digital commodities," and the SEC will regulate "restricted digital assets." The bill also establishes a temporary registration path before the SEC and CFTC finalize their rules, and stipulates that once the network achieves decentralization, related assets can be transformed from securities into digital goods. Although the momentum has weakened after the government shutdown, the market still has high expectations for comprehensive crypto legislation to be introduced in 2026. (The bill is currently underway.) 3. RWA, Tokenization, and New Frontiers In 2025, Real-World Asset Tokenization (RWA) also continued to advance. Unlike native crypto assets, RWA tokenization typically involves moving existing traditional assets onto the blockchain, sometimes with fractionalization. Tokenized assets are diverse, including precious metals or other commodities, U.S. Treasury bonds, and private equity fund shares. This area has entered a new frontier when Nasdaq recently submitted a proposal to the SEC requesting permission for investors to trade existing equity securities in tokenized form. This proposal has sparked considerable discussion, and the SEC has signaled its willingness to consider allowing the trading of existing traditional publicly traded securities in tokenized form. We will continue to strive to keep LPs and the wider readership informed as these structural changes and new initiatives unfold. We also anticipate new developments in digital asset policy and regulation in 2026.

data.title: NYSE's entry: The end of stock tokenization or a spring for brokerages?
data.description:
Author: Zixi.bnb Source: X, @ZixiStablestock

Just now, the NYSE announced that it will be launching a 24/7 on-chain stock trading platform. Simply put: US stocks will soon be available for trading on-chain around the clock.

NYSE will launch tokenization stock trading platform

Many people's first reaction is: "Great! Stocks are finally going to be fully on-chain!" "Does this mean anyone can issue stock tokens?"

But if you really break this down, you'll find a very counterintuitive conclusion:

NYSE The outcome doesn't mean stock tokenization has become more liberal; rather, it means the era of private companies recklessly issuing stock tokens may be coming to an end. First, let's put it simply: What is "Tokenization Stock"? Let's avoid technical jargon and use the most straightforward analogy. Stock: You hold a "share" in a company through a brokerage firm. Tokenization: Through the brokerage firm's infrastructure, users/institutions mint this "share" into tokens on the blockchain. Sounds great, right? Stablestock had the same idea in the middle of the year: Could Stablestock follow the Stablecoin model, using a brokerage-based underlying mechanism to tokenize stocks and allow them to be freely traded on-chain? The problem lies here. Numerous compliance and technical issues arise. I can give some examples: Regarding compliance, without your own brokerage, you lack custody of user assets, preventing users from transferring shares to a brokerage. This means users can only buy from scratch. Technically, consider stock splits. After issuing tokens, complex operations like stock splits (which frequently occur in the stock market) become difficult for smart contracts to perform. Improper oracle operation can lead to liquidation of users in trading products like PERP/Lending. In the months we explored stock tokenization, besides the above issues, we encountered many technical challenges, making us realize that the underlying mechanism for stock tokenization is DTCC or Nasdaq/NYSE, not the issuing company. **If the NYSE/Nasdaq/DTCC don't solve the underlying problems, then stock tokenization will be a dead end.** II. Why can private companies issue stablecoins, but not stock tokens? Unlike stablecoins, stock tokens are not something private companies can issue arbitrarily. **Stablecoins can be issued by private companies because "the US dollar itself is freely circulating"; stock tokens cannot because "stocks are not truly controlled by brokerages or companies."** Stablecoins are pegged to the US dollar. The US dollar itself is a freely circulating asset; as long as you have a bank account, you can receive, pay, and transfer money. Issuing stablecoins is essentially just "acceptance": a user gives you $1, you give them 1 stablecoin on the blockchain; they can exchange the stablecoin for $1 at any time. This logic holds true as long as the reserves are genuine and redemption is reliable. The US dollar doesn't involve dividends, voting, or "ownership registration," making its technical and legal structure relatively simple. Stocks, however, are completely different. Stocks don't reside with a single brokerage firm; their final registration and custody are centralized in a system like DTCC. The stock you buy represents your status as a company shareholder, not an asset that can be transferred at will. Transferring stocks in and out requires clearing, reconciliation, and registration updates—far more complex than a simple transfer. More importantly, things constantly happen to stocks during their ownership, such as dividends, voting, stock splits, and rights issues. Every change must be legally valid and accurately reflected in the shareholder register. This means that issuing stock tokens isn't a "one-and-done" process; it involves responsibility for the entire lifecycle of the stock. We can illustrate this with examples like transfers in and out and stock splits. From the perspective of transferring in and out, all you need is a bank account. This is because the inflow and outflow of US dollars naturally goes through the banking system; transfers don't require notification to anyone; and there's no need to update any "ownership register." However, stocks are not "money"; they possess a complete legal relationship and ownership system. But the actual place where stocks are held isn't with the brokerage firm. Many people think that if they buy stocks through a brokerage app, the stocks are with that brokerage firm. Actually, no. The final registration and custody of stocks are centralized at DTCC (see the flowchart for stocks/funds below). The company's shareholder list, stock splits and consolidations, and voting are still based on DTCC. Unlike money, the transfer of stocks means a change in ownership; the shareholder list needs to be updated, and dividend rights and voting rights also change accordingly. This is not as simple as a bank transfer; different brokerage firms need to reconcile accounts, the clearing system needs to confirm, and the central custody system needs to register. Therefore, stocks are never an asset that can be freely traded. The business logic is completely different from stablecoins.

Broker asset flow and custody

The asset behavior is also completely different. Dollars can simply be left untouched. But stocks pay dividends, vote, undergo stock splits and consolidations, mergers, and new share issuances. Let's take a real-world example: a stock split. Netflix announced a 1-for-10 stock split on November 17th. Suppose a user's stock issuer has 1000 shares of NFLX stock in the brokerage's inventory (registered in DTCC), and 1000 NFLX tokens are circulating on-chain before the stock split. When a 1-for-10 split occurs, the stock automatically becomes 10000 shares on the brokerage without any operation; everything is handled by the clearing and custody system. But what happens on-chain? It seems simple: if 9000 NFLX tokens are forcibly issued on-chain, each existing user's 1 token automatically becomes 10 tokens. But the questions are: who executes this operation? Who ensures that every address is processed correctly? What if users have their tokens in DeFi, lending, or AMMs? How are tokens locked in smart contracts split? Who can guarantee that the oracle will process the price quotes in a timely manner (if completely relying on off-chain quotes, the off-chain quote might be 10, but the on-chain price is actually 100)? If the tokens aren't split, and only the exchange ratio is changed (i.e., 1 token equals 10 shares), the price system could easily become chaotic instantly. There would inevitably be discrepancies between on-chain and off-chain transactions, leading to distortion. Every company action would require changing the rules. This is actually a very complex and frequently occurring phenomenon.

Netflix split 1 for 10 on November 17th

From the above examples, you can see that whether it's transferring in or out, or stock splits and consolidations, the most important infrastructure is actually DTCC and NYSE/Nasdaq, not the stock token company issuer. ...

III. The Rules Change with the NYSE's Entry

When the NYSE officially entered the field of stock tokenization, it wasn't just about adding another "participant," but rather signified a fundamental shift in the industry's focus.

In the early stages, stock tokenization relied heavily on private projects for exploration: project teams issued tokens to map stock value, attempting to solve issues related to trading time, cross-border transactions, and efficiency. However, this model presupposed that there wasn't a widely recognized and sufficiently authoritative "official version" in the market.

The NYSE's entry precisely changed this.

Once a stock tokenization solution supported by top-tier exchanges, clearing systems, and regulatory frameworks emerges, the market's choice will become very pragmatic: most clearinghouses, brokerages, and users will directly connect to the official system rather than continue using privately issued stock tokens. The reason is not complicated—official solutions are inherently more complete in their underlying capabilities. These official stock tokens often directly connect to mature clearing and custody systems, naturally supporting complex corporate behaviors such as stock splits, consolidation, dividends, voting, mergers and acquisitions, and share issuances. These are precisely the areas where privately issued solutions have long struggled to improve and are most prone to problems. For institutions, the completeness of functionality and the clarity of legal responsibilities are far more important than whether it is "on-chain native." More importantly, official endorsement itself creates an attraction for liquidity. When clearinghouses, market makers, banks, and large institutions all provide services around official tokens, privately issued stock tokens will inevitably face problems such as insufficient liquidity, pricing discounts, and excessively high trust costs. Even if it continues technically, it will gradually lose its economic significance. The essence of private companies issuing stock tokens is actually building a side pool outside the massive liquidity of traditional exchanges. Therefore, the NYSE's entry into the market does not represent a "comprehensive boom in stock tokenization," but rather a very real signal: stock tokenization is moving from "multiple experiments in parallel" to "high concentration and standardization." In this landscape, opportunities no longer belong to projects that "issue more tokens," but to those participants who can successfully integrate into the official stock token system and build user portals and trading experiences around it. This is the real change happening in the industry after the NYSE's entry.

IV. Each upgrade in the underlying structure of stocks leads to a paradigm shift for securities firms

If we look back at the history of stock trading over the past 100 years, we will find a very clear pattern: every shift in trading paradigms gives rise to a new type of securities firm.

The first major turning point occurred before the 1970s. At that time, stock trading relied entirely on paper certificates and human intermediaries, making it almost impossible for ordinary people to participate. The stock market was essentially a game for the elite. This is what we often see in old movies: brokers in trading halls completing transactions through open bidding.

The second turning point occurred after the 1970s. With the establishment of DTC (Direct-to-Trade Commission), stock trading began to be centrally processed by large investment banks and securities firms. Institutions such as Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, and Merrill Lynch began to represent clients in completing transactions and clearing.

This is precisely the era described by The Wolf of Wall Street: stock trading remains a professional activity, but it has become accessible to a wider client base via telephone. The third turning point occurred after the 2000s. The widespread adoption of the internet and API-based trading fundamentally altered the barriers to entry in the stock market. The rise of online brokerages like Interactive Brokers and Robinhood marked the first time stock trading truly became mainstream. History has repeatedly demonstrated that once a systemic change occurs in the trading model, the brokerage ecosystem is inevitably reshaped. We believe that by around 2026, stock tokenization will become an irreversible trend. As settlement and delivery gradually migrate to blockchain infrastructure, the entire stock trading system will usher in a new window of reconstruction. The stock tokenization system upgrade initiated by the NYSE, along with the stablecoin settlement system, represents a paradigm shift. And companies like Stablestock, by gradually betting on becoming "crypto-native brokerages" in the second half of 2025, are essentially betting on the continued global penetration of stablecoins. Stablecoins will, for the first time, allow an extremely large population, long excluded from the traditional financial system, to participate in global stock trading with lower barriers to entry and less friction. We believe this will reshape the next generation of brokerages.

V. Stablestock 1-2 Year Roadmap

We have decided to focus our core efforts over the next 12–24 months on building a more crypto-friendly, more on-chain native next-generation brokerage, neobroker.

... Imagine a future where, within the same Broker App, users can not only settle with stablecoins, but also: High-leverage spot trading (launching in June) Perp (launching in H1) Option (launching in September) A combined Crypto asset + stock cross-margin system Prediction market and simpler binary options IPO (launching in March) Hong Kong stock trading (launching in March) Stock Lending

  • Second-level deposit and withdrawal settlement

    • All of this is built on a unified crypto-friendly broker.

    Furthermore, as this foundation continues to mature, we will also release complete developer documentation to empower independent developers to build their own applications based on StableBroker, such as: Lending Markets, AI Trading, Wealth Management Vault, Follow Trading, Onchain ETF, Stocktoken Backed Stablecoin, and more innovative StockFi products. Looking ahead, there is still a long way to go to truly build a complete and mature stock tokenization brokerage infrastructure. VI. In Conclusion The fate of the NYSE will indeed impact some cryptonative stock token projects. Those that previously relied on "private issuance" and "undefined rules" will face higher standards, stricter comparisons, and are more likely to be marginalized. However, this does not mean it is a systemic negative. On the contrary, it is more like a structural reshuffling brought about by industry maturation. When stock tokenization is incorporated into a more complete clearing system and official framework, the real beneficiaries will not be projects issuing more assets, but rather those sectors building infrastructure around trading, settlement, and capital flows. Stablecoins will become a more important entry point for funds; contracts and derivatives will have a clearer and more credible underlying asset; and crypto-friendly brokers will become a key bridge connecting the traditional securities system and the on-chain world. Competition will undoubtedly intensify, but this doesn't mean innovation will disappear. On the contrary, innovation will become more pragmatic: shifting from "how to issue assets" to "how to make assets more efficiently used"; from pursuing mere on-chain formalities to resolving friction issues for real users in depositing, trading, settling, and holding assets. If past stock tokenization was an experiment exploring boundaries, then with the NYSE's exit, the industry is entering a new phase—clearer rules, more professional participants, and innovation more closely aligned with real financial needs. For projects that truly understand the logic of both finance and crypto, this is not the end, but a new beginning.

data.title: Trump's First Year in Office: Memorable Moments and Terrible Deeds
data.description:
Author: Lin Wanwan

January 20, 2026 marks the first anniversary of Trump's inauguration.

Back a year ago, 72 hours before the inauguration, a crypto wallet codenamed 6QSc2Cx began buying large quantities of a new token at 18 cents each. This token had just been created a few hours earlier and had not been publicly promoted.

A few hours later, Trump announced on social media that he had launched a cryptocurrency named after himself, $TRUMP.

After the announcement, the token price surged from less than $1 to $75 within 48 hours.

The wallet that entered early sold off at the peak, making a profit of $109 million. The New York Times, after commissioning an on-chain analytics firm, discovered that this wasn't an isolated case of a "mysterious prophet." On the same day, an American truck driver with the online name Mike invested his daughter's entire college fund in Trump tokens. He posted on overseas media: "The president won't let us lose money." Three weeks later, he lost $47,000. Six months later, the team that issued the token had cashed out over $1 billion. Trump said six years ago, "Cryptocurrency is entirely based on air." He was right. He didn't finish his sentence: the price of air energy depends on who's selling it. No one knows who's on the other end of the wallet. No one knows how they knew in advance. But one thing is certain: as these people leave, another group is entering. Chainalysis data shows that 810,000 wallets lost money on the Trump token, totaling over $2 billion. The average loss was $2,500 per person. Nearly half of these people were newbies who created their first crypto wallet on the day of the token launch. They saw the news of the "president issuing a coin," downloaded the app, and transferred their savings. Further analysis by Chainalysis shows that these new wallets held their positions for an average of 47 hours. They bought near the peak and sold after the price had halved. On-chain data reconstructed a typical path: download the app → deposit funds → buy → bullish → add to position → crash → sell at a loss → uninstall the app. The entire process took less than a week. Someone posted on Reddit: "I used my daughter's college fund." The top-rated reply was: "Bro, the president won't let you lose money." The president didn't actually make everyone lose money; after all, many people earned life-changing money that day. It's just that not most people made money. Someone posted a rocket emoji on Twitter with the caption "TRUMP to the moon." Eleven days later, he posted again: "Fed up with this TRUMP garbage, sold it all." When he sold, the token was priced between $24 and $27. If he had held it for a few more months, he would have seen the price drop below $5, a drop of over 90% from its peak. Meanwhile, the two companies associated with the Trump family that issued the $TRUMP token earned over $320 million in transaction fees in its first year. This doesn't even include the 800 million tokens they locked, worth tens of billions of dollars at the issue price. Interestingly, the token's purchase terms include a small line: "Purchasers agree to waive their right to participate in any class-action lawsuits." Another line states: "This token is 'not an investment opportunity' and 'is not related to any political activity or government position.'" In other words: If you buy it and lose money, you can't sue me. The money I make has nothing to do with being president. $TRUMP is just an appetizer. The real main course is World Liberty Financial. This is a decentralized finance platform, founded in September 2024 by the Trump family and several partners. The platform issues a governance token called WLFI and a stablecoin called USD1. The equity structure is as follows: the Trump family holds 60%. 75% of the net proceeds from token sales go to the family. The USD1 stablecoin's reserve assets are invested in US Treasury bonds, generating approximately $80 million in interest annually, 75% of which also goes to the family. In other words, this is not a project "supported" by Trump, nor is it a project he "endorsed." It is a project directly owned and profited from by the Trump family. As of the end of 2025, World Liberty had raised over $550 million in funding. The list of investors reads like an Interpol watchlist: MGX, the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund led by members of the UAE royal family, invested $2 billion in Binance's USD1 stablecoin in May 2025. This means that UAE government funds flowed into the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange through a stablecoin issued by the Trump family. Why would these people invest in a crypto project of a US president's family? Reuters interviewed several investors, and the answers were surprisingly consistent: "Close to the president." There's an old Wall Street joke: How much is it worth to play a round of golf with the president? The answer is: It depends on whether you're playing sports or paying lawyer fees. World Liberty has rewritten the answer to this joke. Now there are price tags: WLFI tokens start at $250,000. "Platinum Seats" are $1 million. "Founding Partners" are $20 million. You're not buying tokens. You're buying a photo opportunity, a dinner, a name that will be remembered. In political science, this is called "access capitalism." Previously, it was hidden in super PACs, charity dinners, and lobbying firm bills. Now it's written in smart contracts, traded 24/7, and globally accessible. The Democratization of Corruption. A financial commentator put it more bluntly: "Eric Trump was pitching a $20 million token package in Dubai while his father was simultaneously formulating US crypto policy. You call that a business model? I call it a pay-to-play channel." Clearing the Field. But this pay-to-play channel has one prerequisite: no one can investigate. So the first thing Trump did after taking office was to eliminate everyone who might investigate him. The speed and efficiency were nothing short of an art. First, there was a personnel purge. On his inauguration day, SEC Chairman Gary Gensler resigned. This "crypto hunter" sued almost every major exchange during his tenure. His successor, Paul Atkins, was a consultant for the crypto industry association. The newly formed SEC "Crypto Task Force" is led by Hester Peirce, nicknamed "Crypto Mom" ​​in the industry, who has opposed regulation for years. Following this was the clearing of cases. Cases from the Gensler era were dropped one after another. The Coinbase case was dropped. The Ripple case was dropped. The Kraken case was dropped. The OpenSea investigation was terminated. The Uniswap investigation was terminated. The Robinhood investigation was terminated. According to statistics from The New York Times, the SEC's drop rate for crypto cases is 33%, while for other cases it is 4%. This disparity is unprecedented in the SEC's history. Finally, the organization was disbanded. On April 7, 2025, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche signed a memorandum announcing the immediate disbandment of the "National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team." This team was established in 2021 to investigate crypto money laundering, hacking, and fraud. Blanche wrote in the memo, "The Department of Justice is not a digital asset regulator." What he didn't write was that at the time of signing the memo, he personally held more than $150,000 in crypto assets. Blanche was later asked about this when testifying before Congress. He said, "My crypto holdings were 'compliantly disclosed.'" He's right. Disclosure equals compliance. Compliance eliminates the conflict of interest. This is the brilliance of this system: it doesn't require hiding conflicts of interest; it only requires turning them into a table. Within three months, the person was replaced, the case was dropped, and the investigating institutions were dissolved. The referee doesn't just play football; he dismantles the field. The price list for amnesty. This business is missing one last piece: trust. Trump's crypto empire needs to attract global funding and restore the dignity of those with criminal records. They have money, resources, and connections, but they are either "confessed criminals" or "defendants being prosecuted." What to do? Amnesty. On January 21, 2025, Trump, on his second day in office, signed his first crypto-related pardon. The pardoned person was Ross Ulbricht, founder of the Silk Road darknet marketplace, who had been sentenced to two life sentences plus 40 years in prison for operating a platform that facilitated a $1 billion drug deal. Court records show that at least six people died after purchasing drugs from that platform. Trump wrote on Truth Social: "Those who prosecuted him are scum." After his release from prison, Ulbricht appeared on stage at the 2025 Bitcoin conference, telling the cheering crowd: "I was in jail a few months ago, and now I'm free. Thank you, thank you Trump." In March, four founders of BitMEX were pardoned after pleading guilty to violating anti-money laundering laws and being described by prosecutors as operators of a "money laundering platform." In October, Binance founder Changpeng Zhao was pardoned after pleading guilty in 2023 to allowing the platform to be used for money laundering. These three pardons, totaling six people, spanned dark web drug trafficking, money laundering, and illegal business operations. All liquidated within ten months. But what's even more noteworthy are those who weren't pardoned. Sam Bankman-Fried, founder of FTX, was sentenced to 25 years in prison in 2024 for fraud, causing clients to lose $8 billion. He donated $5.2 million to the Biden campaign in 2020. No pardon. Do Kwon, founder of Terra/Luna, was sentenced to 15 years in prison in December 2025 for designing an algorithmic stablecoin that caused investors to lose $40 billion. No pardon. In terms of the severity of their crimes, SBF and Do Kwon caused far more damage than those who were pardoned. Legally, the FTX case was a clear case of customer fund fraud. What's the difference? Those who were pardoned either invested in Trump's projects, had companies with business dealings with Trump, or had significant influence in the crypto community to endorse him. Those who weren't pardoned either donated to the Democratic Party or had no business relationship with Trump. This is a price list for pardons. It's not written on paper, it's written in judgments, pardons, and on the walls of the cells of those who continue to serve their sentences. The real function of a pardon isn't to absolve punishment. The punishment is over. Ross Ulbricht served 11 years, Changpeng Zhao served 4 months, and the founder of BitMEX paid a $100 million fine. The function of a pardon is to send signals. Signal one: I will protect those who cooperate with me. Signal two: For those who don't cooperate with me, look at Sam Bankman-Fried. Signal three: I set the rules, and I can change them. Follow the organization, and the organization will protect you. Trump wrote this into the Federal Register. A Corruption Production Line. Is this corruption? Of course not. Corruption is clandestine, it's hidden, and it will be investigated. This is a well-designed system. Every component is legal, every transaction is recorded on the blockchain, and every disclosure is written in government documents. It doesn't need to be hidden. It was designed not to be hidden. Traditional corruption is like a workshop. You have to find a middleman, launder money, worry about being recorded, and worry about witnesses turning against you. Every transaction is risky. This system is an assembly line. Token contracts automatically distribute revenue, the blockchain automatically records, and disclosure forms automatically comply with regulations. There are no middlemen, no cash, no witnesses. Only code. The code doesn't betray you. The code doesn't lie. The code only runs as designed. And the person who designed it is also the person who designed the rules. Trump's genius lies not in corruption. Anyone can be corrupt. His genius lies in designing corruption as a product. Bribery becomes "investment." Accepting bribes becomes "dividends." Pardons become "judicial reform." Regulatory withdrawal becomes "support for innovation." Everything is written in the terms, recorded on the blockchain, legal, compliant, open, and transparent. In 2019, Trump said cryptocurrency was "completely based on air." He's right. He just forgot the second half of the sentence: air can be sold for money, as long as the seller can decide what's legal. The system is still running. Tokens are still being traded, stablecoins are still earning interest, and money from all over the world is still pouring into those few wallet addresses bearing Trump's name. And those 810,000 retail investors who lost money, those newbies who rushed in after seeing "the president issuing a coin," those who thought buying Trump coins was patriotic. They weren't investors. They were fuel. Casinos don't thank gamblers. They just drain them dry. Someone might ask: Is this legal? That question is outdated. In this game, "legal" isn't a description, but a product feature. Just as the iPhone has waterproofing, this system has a "legal" feature. It's designed to be legal, just as it's designed to make money. The real question isn't "Is this legal?" The real question is: what does the word "legal" mean when the person defining "legal" and the person profiting from it are the same person? In 2019, Trump said cryptocurrency was based on air. In 2025, he proved himself: air can be priced, traded, and can make a president a billionaire. The only prerequisite is that you have to be the one who decides what "air" is.

data.title: Qianwen Agent and Doubao Mobile: A blue ocean market is emerging where agents sell shovels.
data.description:
Author: Guo Xiaojing, Editor: Xu Qingyang

Agents are hailed as the native application form of the AI ​​era and are highly anticipated. Gold diggers for the AI ​​era will flock to them.

From the end of 2025 to the present, agents capable of "getting things done" have garnered significant attention.
