Examples include the Doubao mobile phone, which was resold for tens of thousands of yuan on the second-hand market; Manus, a general-purpose intelligent agent startup acquired by Meta for billions of dollars; and Qianwen Agent, which announced its integration with over 400 products across Alibaba's ecosystem, enabling various long-distance actions such as ordering takeout and booking tickets.

These meticulously crafted intelligent agents have attracted public attention and sparked discussions about AI moving from dialogue to action. Under the spotlight, the open-source community is also witnessing a lively wave of "hand-crafted intelligent agents."

Tech geeks use flexible toolkits like Claude Skills to assemble "digital employees" tailored to specific business needs, or create agents specifically designed to optimize workflows. It's like cultivating a dedicated digital partner in the AI ​​era; the sense of control and accomplishment from building something from scratch is what truly makes it appealing. A senior tech industry analyst summarized: From the perspective of the agent industry, the market is differentiated into three layers: the "foundation capability layer," the "AI-oriented business layer," and the "human-oriented business layer." This is an exciting qualitative change, containing immense potential. The most noteworthy is the "machine (AI)-oriented business layer," a completely new market segmentation that didn't exist in the PC internet or mobile internet eras. Within this layer, an infrastructure market specifically serving agents is rapidly taking shape. Those "shovel sellers" who provide search APIs and identity authentication for agents have already quietly entered the market, seizing this new blue ocean.

01 Starting with the wildly popular Claude Skills

In mid-January, with the emergence of Claude Cowork, Skills completely broke down the barriers of developers. SKILL.md, which was originally only active in the command line, entered the desktops of every ordinary employee.

02 The Initial Formation of the Intelligent Agent "Shovel" Ecosystem

The explosive popularity of Skills in early 2026 only revealed the tip of the iceberg of the entire intelligent agent economy. Its ecosystem potential is enormous, but within the entire ecosystem foundation, there are many more crucial components.

What is a complete framework for an Agent? OpenAI researcher Lilian Weng defines an Agent as an intelligent system that uses a large model as its "brain," planning and breaking down goals, memorizing and accumulating experience, and using tools to expand boundaries, thereby achieving autonomous execution of complex tasks. This is a widely accepted standard framework in the industry.

The "brain" layer is currently the domain of basic model players. However, future competition is full of variables because the industry is facing a new challenge: whether end-to-end large models are themselves intelligent agents. We will focus on breaking down this point of contention in the last part of the article.

With a brain, agents also need to learn planning, the art of breaking down complex goals into actionable steps. Intelligent agents in 2026 will generally possess the ability of "reflection and self-examination." They will check their output like humans, and when they find that the search results do not match, they will automatically correct the path through patterns such as ReAct.

... While orchestration frameworks like LangGraph or CrewAI are invisible to ordinary users, they act as built-in "meta-tools" for agents, ensuring tasks don't get stuck in logical loops by monitoring and optimizing execution paths. The key to enabling agents to truly personalize services for users lies in "memory." Beyond short-term memory that records the current conversation, long-term memory built through RAG (Retrieval Augmentation) technology has become standard. Using vector database APIs like Pinecone or Milvus, agents can retrieve information from massive amounts of historical documents at any time. The most significant trend in 2026 is the rise of "personalized profiles"—agents will not only remember your work preferences but also access your Google Drive or database records across platforms via MCP, forming a warm, personalized digital memory that is no longer "disappearing after reading." What truly sets Agent apart is its "hands and feet," namely, its "tools and actions." This is currently the most thriving tool layer, boasting a vast ecosystem from Tavily's dedicated search API to the Zapier automated integration platform. The emergence of Claude Skills, in particular, encapsulates complex workflows into reusable capability packages. Coupled with the MCP standard protocol, Agent can seamlessly integrate with GitHub, Slack, and even local Docker environments, directly operating over 8,000 SaaS applications. In 2026, Anthropic further supplemented the missing interaction logic in the MCP protocol with Advanced Tool Use, utilizing tool search, usage examples, and programmatic calls to solve the perception overload and decision paralysis faced by Agents when dealing with a massive number of tools. Building a production-grade Agent is now as standardized as assembling Lego bricks. In simple terms, developers first define clear "personas" to set behavioral styles; then they build an "environment" by connecting various external APIs through the MCP connector; next, they inject "knowledge" to store business documents in a vector database; then they design the "orchestration" logic using an orchestration framework; and finally, they set up a security filtering mechanism called "guardrails" at the outermost layer to ensure that the agent's behavior is always safe and controllable.

Note: The above layered framework is compiled based on industry analysis and publicly available information. Currently, there is no unified industrial standard.

A prototype of an intelligent agent ecosystem has begun to take shape. However, the industry is still in its early stages, and there are still many gray areas and challenges to be overcome.

03. The Divergence in the Agent Ecosystem

The first divergence lies in the essence of Agents: whether they help people complete processes or become another kind of "person."

One ​​school of thought consists of players like Dify, n8n, or LangChain, who are deeply involved in "workflow orchestration." In this paradigm, developers are like seasoned watchmakers, meticulously manipulating nodes in the LangGraph, considering what to do in the first step, what to do in the second step, and so on.

The other school of thought believes that Agent creators need to define the boundaries and behavioral rules of atomic operations as if building a physical world, and then embrace the possibility of Agents generating "magic" in uncertainty.

Just as Manus provides a pre-built toolkit through Sandboxes, the Agent, while receiving human instructions, autonomously discovers and combines atomic operations within its environment. This perspective suggests that a true Agent should "improve" in its environment, like a human, rather than rigidly adhering to a pre-defined process. This difference in understanding directly extends to the underlying logic of the agent economy: **What is the smallest settlement unit in this ecosystem?** One school of thought "breaks down" large goals into countless small sub-tasks, believing that as long as the breakdown is detailed enough, the Agent can execute them effectively. However, the Agent's brain is based on a large model of the Transformer architecture, which means that it is inherently impossible for an Agent to achieve 100% precise breakdown. Therefore, another trend is shifting towards "Intent Protocols" centered on "generation." **In this concept, intent is the smallest economic unit. Agents don't need to focus on complex intermediate steps; instead, they directly drive the release of capabilities through intent.** Mingke, an entrepreneur in the Agent field, built the Agency Framework: "In my framework, the Agent is the interface for all capabilities. Other capabilities are exposed to end users through this Agent interface, including access control, identity verification, and state management (of capability usage), such as whether the goal has been achieved." This approach means that future users may no longer be aware of specific apps or databases; they will only interact with the entire digital world through the "veil" of the Agent. According to traditional thinking, this paradigm poses a fundamental challenge to the existing internet ecosystem architecture. This is why, although Doubao Mobile has lofty ideals, it faces numerous obstacles in actual implementation; Qianwen Agent, within Alibaba's own ecosystem framework, is able to complete user-assigned tasks relatively completely. In the traditional world, attempting to establish new rules of the game is bound to be fraught with difficulties. Many agent entrepreneurs believe that successfully enabling agents to fulfill intentions requires a new approach to a new world. In this vision, the smallest unit of the agent economy, as mentioned above, is "intention." **Intention is defined as "the desired state."** After obtaining this state described by the user, the agent transforms the vague natural language into a series of executable plans. For example, a user saying "I want to go on a business trip to Beijing" actually includes a series of intentions such as booking tickets, booking hotels, and checking the weather. Ordering a cup of milk tea is also an intention. Future business settlements will be based on paying users for "achieving their intentions." However, there is still a significant hurdle between "understanding intentions" and "delivering value": How can intentions be standardized? What constitutes a true achievement of an intention? Currently, agents are still like isolated islands, and a universal protocol for expressing intentions has not yet been established within the ecosystem. This means that agents cannot yet directly settle value based on intentions like Visa or SWIFT systems handle currency. Clearly, agents still have a long way to go before truly achieving value interconnection.

04 Models and Intelligent Agents: Where is the Boundary?

Another core issue that has always been controversial is: if a basic model with agent capabilities can implement agent functions end-to-end, where is the boundary between it and the agent? If an end-to-end model can handle everything, can the ecosystem of intelligent agents still develop healthily?

04 Models and Intelligent Agents: Where is the Boundary?

... Mingke explained, "The essence of LLM is only Next Token Generation. How this token, once issued, is transformed into an Action that can impact the environment is something outside the model itself. Next Token Generation is the only thing LLM can do. Although after productization everyone calls it a model, it's actually a model with many things added on top of it. For example, Claude has started to become agent-based; the kernel model, Claude code, and coworker have been filled with many things, such as virtual machines, which don't belong to the model itself." Therefore, end-to-end models and agents with agent capabilities, if we don't consider the technical implementation path, are actually a single species, both requiring an ecosystem to support the agent. Currently, the gold rush in the agent economy has begun. Compared to the chaotic competition in end-user applications, the "infrastructure layer" that provides standardized capabilities to support agents—the indispensable "shovels"—has evolved into a highly certain and explosive growth sector. However, this time, "selling shovels" is definitely not a secondary role.

data.title: The Clarity Act, the Banks and the Profits Dispute
data.description:
Author: Kolten, Aave Labs; Translated by: Jinse Finance

The CLARITY Act has sparked debate about the future of U.S. money and banking. One of its core provisions would prohibit digital asset service providers (such as cryptocurrency exchanges) from paying revenue to customers simply because they hold “payment stablecoins.”

This proposed ban on third-party platforms follows the 2025 GENIUS Act, which already prohibits stablecoin issuers from paying interest. The banking industry supports these measures to protect its revenue, a lucrative part of its business model. In simple terms, a bank's operating model is to accept customer deposits and pay lower interest rates, then lend those deposits to others at higher interest rates or invest them in assets such as government bonds. A bank's net interest margin, or interest rate spread, is the difference between the interest earned and the interest paid. This model can be very profitable. JPMorgan Chase reported record net profits of $58.5 billion in 2024. Revenue reached $180.6 billion, with net interest income of $92.6 billion, its main source of income. New fintech solutions provide depositors with a more direct way to obtain higher returns, while also introducing competition that the industry has historically avoided. It is not surprising that some large traditional banks are using regulatory tools to protect their business models, as this strategy is reasonable and has precedents. Dual-mode banking. As of early 2026, the national average annual interest rate for savings accounts was 0.47%. Meanwhile, some of the largest U.S. banks, including JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America, offer a standard APY yield of 0.01% on basic savings accounts. During the same period, the yield on risk-free investments is the 3-month U.S. Treasury bond yield, which is approximately 3.6%. Therefore, a large bank could attract customer deposits, purchase government bonds, and earn a spread of over 3.5% with minimal risk. JPMorgan Chase has approximately $2.4 trillion in deposits, and theoretically, it could generate over $85 billion in revenue from deposit interest rate spreads alone. While this is an oversimplified estimate, the principle still holds true. Since the global financial crisis, the banking industry has diverged into two distinct types of institutions: low-interest-rate banks and high-interest-rate banks. Low-interest-rate banks are large, traditional banks that leverage their extensive branch networks and brand recognition to attract deposits from interest-rate-insensitive customers. High-interest-rate banks, like Goldman Sachs' Marcus or Ally Bank, typically operate online and compete on price by offering deposit rates closer to market rates. Research data from Kundu, Muir, and Zhang shows that the gap between the 75th and 25th percentiles of deposit rates at the top 25 banks has widened from 0.70% in 2006 to over 3.5% today. The business model of low-interest-rate banks is profitable because it relies on a group of depositors who are not actively seeking higher returns. "$6 Trillion Deposit Flight" Banking groups believe that allowing stablecoins to generate yields will lead to a "deposit flight" of up to $6.6 trillion. They claim this will cause a credit crunch in the economy. Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan expressed this concern at an investor conference in January 2026, warning, "Deposits are not just conduits; they are funds. If deposits flow out of banks, lending capacity will shrink, and banks may have to rely more on wholesale funding, which will incur costs." He added that Bank of America itself would not be affected, but small and medium-sized enterprises would be the first to feel the impact. This argument views deposits flowing into stablecoins as a departure from the commercial banking system. However, this is not always the case. When a customer buys a stablecoin, the dollar is transferred to the stablecoin issuer, who holds it as reserves. For example, the reserves of USDC, the main stablecoin issued by Circle, are managed by BlackRock and are held in a combination of cash and short-term U.S. Treasury securities. These deposits remain within the traditional financial system, meaning the total amount of deposits doesn't necessarily change; they are simply redistributed from customer accounts to stablecoin issuers' accounts. What's the real problem? What the banking industry is truly worried about is the flow of deposits from low-interest-rate accounts to other investment products with higher returns. For example, Coinbase's USDC rewards and DeFi products like the Aave App offer yields far exceeding those of most banks. For customers, the choice is between earning 0.01% on $1 held at a large bank or earning over 4% on the same $1 held in stablecoins—a difference of over 400 times. This dynamic challenges the low-interest-rate banking model, prompting customers to move funds from trading accounts to interest-bearing accounts and making depositors more sensitive to interest rates. In a world with yield-yielding stablecoins, customers can access market rates without changing their primary bank accounts, which will intensify existing competition among banks. As fintech analyst Scott Johnson puts it, "Banks aren't actually competing with stablecoins for deposits; they're competing with each other. Stablecoins simply accelerate this dynamic, ultimately benefiting consumers." Research by Kundu, Muir, and Zhang supports this view, finding that when market interest rates rise, deposits tend to flow from low-interest-rate banks to high-interest-rate banks. This flow of funds facilitates the issuance of personal and commercial loans, and high-interest-rate banks are increasingly issuing such loans. Yield-paying stablecoins are likely to replicate this effect, channeling funds to more competitive institutions. Historical Similarities The current conflict surrounding stablecoin yields bears a resemblance to historical conflicts surrounding stablecoin yields. Regulation Q, enacted during the Great Depression, aimed to limit banks' interest rates on deposits to prevent "excessive competition." For decades, this regulation had little impact as market interest rates remained below the statutory cap. But by the 1970s, inflation and rising interest rates made these caps binding. For much of the 1960s, the federal funds rate had been below 5%, but then rose sharply, peaking at 20% in March 1980, when laws prohibited banks from offering competitive rates. In 1971, Bruce Bent and Henry Brown created the first money market mutual fund, the Reserve Fund, which offered depositors market interest rate returns along with checkbook functionality. Today, agreements like this abound. Aave functions similarly, allowing users to earn deposit returns without bank intermediaries. These funds grew from 76 funds with $45 billion in assets in 1979 to 159 funds with a total size exceeding $180 billion in just two years, and now manage over $8 trillion in assets. Banks and regulators initially opposed the move. These regulations were ultimately deemed unfair to depositors, prompting Congress to pass legislation in 1980 and 1982 to phase out interest rate caps.

The Rise of Stablecoins

The stablecoin market is also expanding at a similar pace, with its total market capitalization growing from just over $4 billion at the beginning of 2020 to over $300 billion by 2026. The largest stablecoin, Tether (USDT), will reach a market capitalization of $186 billion in 2026. This expansion indicates market demand for a freely circulating digital dollar that could potentially offer competitive yields.

The Rise of Stablecoins

The stablecoin market is also expanding at a similar pace, with its total market capitalization growing from just over $4 billion at the beginning of 2020 to over $300 billion by 2026. The largest stablecoin, Tether (USDT), will reach a market capitalization of $186 billion by 2026. This expansion indicates market demand for a digital dollar that can be freely circulated and potentially offers competitive yields.

The debate over stablecoin yields is a modern version of the money market fund debate. Banks opposing stablecoin yields are primarily vested interests who have benefited from the existing low-interest-rate system. Their goal is to protect their business models from a technology that could bring greater value to consumers. Markets tend to adopt technologies that offer better solutions over time, and the role of regulators is to decide whether to facilitate or delay this shift.

data.title: 2026 AI Agent Economic Outlook: Reshaping AI Identity and the Flow of Network Value
data.description:
Author: Kevin, Movemaker Researcher; Source: X, @MovemakerCN

Introduction: A Structural Leap from Generative AI to “Agent Behavior”

In 2026, the field of artificial intelligence will experience a structural leap from “generative capabilities” to “agent action capabilities.” If 2023-2024 was about the amazing language generation capabilities of large language models, then 2026 will mark the formal establishment of the “AI Agent Economy.”

Based on the predictions and analysis of the a16z Crypto research team, further research reveals that 2026 will be a year of deep integration between AI, a productivity tool, and Crypto, a value distribution layer.

AI is no longer merely a passive tool responding to human commands, but an active participant with reasoning, planning, trading, and autonomous discovery capabilities. Based on a16z Crypto's outlook report, the three core trends reshaping the AI+Crypto landscape in 2026 are: A new research paradigm: from single-agent to "Agent-Wrapping-Agent". A revolution in financial infrastructure: from KYC to KYA (Know Your Agent). Economic model reconstruction: addressing the "hidden tax" crisis facing open networks through payment and programmable IP. These three trends are not isolated: the shift in research paradigms relies on advanced collaboration between agents; advanced collaboration requires agents to possess verifiable identities (KYA); and agents with identities must adhere to new value exchange protocols when acquiring data. 1. The New Era of Polymaths: The "Agent-Wrapping-Agent" Architecture in Advanced Research Starting this year, the definition of "AI-assisted research" will undergo a qualitative leap. We will no longer talk about simple literature retrieval or text summarization, but witness AI systems capable of substantive reasoning, hypothesis generation, and even autonomously solving doctoral-level problems. The core driving force behind this transformation lies in the shift from linear suggestion engineering of a single model to complex, recursive AWA workflows. 1.1 Breakthrough in Reasoning Ability: Crossing the Boundaries of Pattern Matching Scott Kominers of a16z points out that AI models are evolving from simply understanding instructions to being able to receive abstract instructions (like guiding a doctoral student) and return novel and correctly executed answers. Recent technological advancements indicate that AI models are breaking through the "random parrot" ceiling, exhibiting slow, deliberate reasoning abilities similar to human "systematic" thinking. 1.1.1 The "Illusion of Usefulness" With the enhancement of reasoning abilities, a new style of "polymath" research is emerging. Scott describes this style as: "Using AI to cross disciplinary boundaries, speculating on potential deep connections between topology and economics, biology and materials science." The "illusionary" nature of large models, often criticized, is being reconstructed as a "generative exploration" mechanism within the context of scientific discovery: Protein Design Case Study: Researchers at the University of Washington used the "whole-family illusion" concept to generate over 1 million unique protein structures not found in nature. Among them, a novel luciferase was screened that exhibited catalytic activity comparable to natural enzymes but with higher substrate specificity. Fluid Dynamics Discovery: Through Physical Information Neural Networks (PINNs), researchers discovered new unstable singularities in Navier-Stokes equations, revealing previously unknown patterns in fluid motion. The core of this research style lies in allowing the model to "think wildly" in the abstract space to generate high-entropy conjectures, which are then filtered using a rigorous logical verifier. 1.2 Detailed Explanation of AWA Architecture To harness this powerful reasoning and generative capability, research workflows are shifting from flattened to hierarchical structures. AWA refers not only to dialogue between multiple agents but also to a recursive, hierarchical control structure. 1.2.1 Orchestrator-Executor Pattern This is currently the most mainstream AWA implementation pattern. A "principal researcher" agent is responsible for maintaining the global context and research objectives, decomposing tasks and distributing them to a dedicated group of "executor" agents. Architectural Advantages: Data from Anthropic shows that a multi-agent system consisting of Claude Opus as the master agent and Claude Sonnet as a sub-agent outperforms a single Claude Opus agent in complex research tasks by 90.2%. This performance improvement is mainly attributed to context isolation—the master agent does not need to process redundant information for each sub-task, thus maintaining the clarity of reasoning.

1.2.2 Recursive Self-Improvement and the MOSAIC Framework

Another key feature of the AWA architecture is the introduction of a Reflexion loop. When the underlying agent fails to execute a task, the error information is fed back to a "critic" agent for analysis and correction.

The MOSAIC framework (Multi-Agent System for AI-driven Code)

By introducing specialized "self-reflection agents" and "principle generation agents," the accuracy of scientific code generation has been significantly improved without relying on verification test cases. This closed loop of "trial and error-reflection-retry" simulates the thought process of human scientists when facing experimental failures. 1.3 Case Study: Sakana AI's "AI Scientist" The most noteworthy AWA application case in 2025 is Sakana AI's "The AI ​​Scientist" system. This is a system designed to fully automate the entire lifecycle of scientific discovery. 1.3.1 Fully Automated Scientific Research Closed-Loop Process The system, based on a starting code template (such as NanoGPT), uses LLM as a "mutation operator" to brainstorm diverse research directions and calls the Semantic Scholar API. Literature review ensures novelty. Experimental Iteration: The "Experimenter" Agent writes and executes code. If the experiment fails, the system captures error logs using the Aider tool and autonomously repairs the code until a visualization is obtained. Paper Writing: The "Writer" Agent uses LaTeX to write a complete scientific paper, covering abstract, methods, experimental results, and autonomously finds cited literature to generate BibTeX. Automated Peer Review: The generated paper is submitted to a simulated "Reviewer" Agent, which scores it according to the standards of top conferences (such as NeurIPS). The system can even make multiple revisions based on reviewer comments. 1.3.2 Economic Benefits and Quality The economic efficiency of the "scientist" system is astonishing: the computational cost of generating a complete research paper is only about $15. The paper generated by this system, "Compositional Regularization," even successfully passed peer review at the ICLR workshop. Although limitations such as citation illusions and logical flaws still exist, this case demonstrates that AI has the ability not only to assist research but also to execute the entire research process. 2. The Command of Identity: From KYC to KYA As agents are given the power to perform tasks and transactions, the digital economy faces an unprecedented identity crisis. Sean Neville (CEO of Catena Labs) warns that the number of "non-human identities" in the financial services sector has reached 96 times the number of human employees, and even as high as 100:1 in some statistics. These agents—without bank accounts, without real-name authentication, yet operating at machine speed—are a huge compliance black hole. The industry is urgently shifting from traditional KYC to... KYA (Know Your Agent). 2.1 The Emergence and Risks of Non-Human Identities (NHI) 2.1.1 “Shadow AI” and the 96:1 Imbalance 45% of financial service institutions acknowledge the existence of unapproved “shadow AI agents” within their organizations. These agents create “identity silos” outside the formal governance framework. Risk Scenarios: A test agent used for cloud resource optimization might autonomously purchase expensive reserved instances without human intervention; or a trading bot might trigger erroneous sell orders during market volatility. Attribution Dilemma: When an agent violates regulations, who is responsible? The engineer who developed it? The manager who deployed it? Or the vendor who provided the underlying model? Without KYA, these responsibilities cannot be defined. 2.2 KYA Framework: The Trust Foundation of the Machine Economy KYA is not just about issuing ID cards, but about establishing a complete digital identity system encompassing subjects, credentials, permissions, and reputation. 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of KYA alt="jR2oyBeNftkjgbuN2nXhBfJyvRnuLBJu5BFcacmH.jpeg">

  1. Subject: The entity legally responsible for the Agent. The agent must be cryptographically linked to a KYC/KYB verified human or business account.

  2. Agent Identity: A unique digital identity based on a decentralized identifier. The DID is cryptographically generated, immutable, and cross-platform portable.

  3. Mandate/Delegation: A statement of authority issued through Verifiable Credentials (VCs). For example, a VC could state: "This Agent is authorized to make purchases on behalf of Alice on Amazon, with a maximum limit of..." "$500".

2.2.2 Cryptographic Binding and Trust Chain

When an Agent initiates a transaction, it presents a VC. The verifier does not need to trust the Agent itself, but only needs to verify whether the digital signature on the VC comes from a trusted issuer. This mechanism creates a "trust chain": Bank trusts enterprise -> Enterprise issues VC to Agent -> Merchant verifies VC -> Transaction passes.

2.3 Protocol Stack Debate: Standardization of Agent Identity

2.3.1 Skyfire and KYAPay Protocol

Skyfire launched the KYAPay open standard, whose core innovation lies in composite tokens:

  • kya token: Contains identity information (such as "verified enterprise agent").

  • pay token: Contains payment capability (such as "pre-authorization of 10 USDC").

  • kya+pay token: Packages identity and payment, allowing the agent to complete "visitor checkout" without manual form filling.

2.3.2 Catena Labs and ACK (Agent Commerce Kit)

Catena Labs, founded by USDC architect Sean, launched ACK, aiming to create "HTTP for intelligent agent commerce". ACK emphasizes the use of W3C DID.

Standards and account abstractions allow agents to directly control on-chain smart contract wallets, achieving stronger security than API keys. 2.3.3 Google AP2 and x402 Extensions Google's Agent Payments Protocol (AP2) uses "authorization letters" to manage permissions and has partnered with Coinbase to develop the AP2 x402 extension, directly integrating crypto payment standards into the protocol. 2.4 Smart Agent Credit Scoring and Risk Control KYA is still the beginning of a reputation system. On-chain Reputation (ERC-7007): Through ERC-7007 (verifiable AI) (Generate content token standards), every successful interaction of the Agent (such as timely payment, generating high-quality code) can be recorded on the chain, forming a verifiable history. Real-time circuit breakers: Financial institutions are deploying AI gateways. If the behavior of a transaction Agent deviates from the benchmark (such as high-frequency abnormal trading), the system can immediately revoke its VC, triggering "digital suppression." 3. Economic Restructuring: Solving the "Hidden Tax" of Open Networks Liz of a16z points out that AI Agents are levying a "hidden tax" on open networks: In order to serve users, Agents massively extract data (context layer) from content websites, but systematically bypass the advertising and subscription models that support the production of this content. If this parasitic relationship is not resolved, it will lead to the depletion of the content ecosystem. 3.1 "The Great Decoupling": The Full Arrival of the Zero-Click Economy In 2025, the digital publishing industry witnessed the "Great Decoupling": search volume rose, but the number of clicks flowing to websites plummeted. 3.1.1 The Brutal Data of Traffic Erosion [Image of a file: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7427173_watermarknone.png] Zero-Click Rate Soars: a16z predicts that traditional search engine traffic will decline by 2026. 25%. Similarweb data shows that the zero-click search rate has risen to 65% in 2025.

  • Click-through rate (CTR) collapse: DMG Media reports that when AI Overview appears above search results, the click-through rate of its content plummets by 89%. Even the top-ranked search result loses 34.5% of clicks in front of AI Summary.

This provides a clear binary signal for the Agent.

  • C2PA (Content Origin and Authenticity Alliance): Proves the original source of content by embedding tamper-proof "content credentials." Even if content is ingested by AI, the cryptographic signature provided by C2PA ensures that the attribution chain remains unbroken, providing a basis for royalty distribution.
